title: Designing explicit functionals for the charge density in terms of a potential
authors: Muhammed Hüseyin Güneş, Ayoub Aouina, Vitaly Gorelov, Matteo Gatti, Lucia Reining
abstract: One of the most powerful strategies to address properties of real many-body systems is to incorporate data obtained for models, for example, to use data of the homogeneous electron gas in order to build the Local Density Approximation for the Kohn-Sham exchange-correlation potential. In the present work, we examine to what extent we can use model data to design functionals directly for observables of materials. In particular, we study different approximations for the charge density of real inhomogeneous materials expressed as a simple, explicit functional of a given Kohn-Sham potential, using as central building block the Lindhard density-density response function of the homogeneous electron gas. Our increasingly realistic set of approximations includes a fully nearsighted expression equivalent to the Thomas-Fermi approximation, functional Taylor expansions, and different approximations to the Connector Theory developed in [Aouina \textit{et al.}, npj Computational Materials {\bf 11}, 242 (2025)]. In all cases, the charge density is obtained without ever solving the Kohn-Sham Schrödinger equation. Results for cubic helium, a prototypical strongly inhomogeneous material, systematically improve with higher levels of approximation, indicating that this is a promising route to obtain expressions that are relatively simple to calculate and to analyze.
summary: This paper proposes a new way to calculate electron density in complex materials by using simple, explicit formulas derived from a model electron gas. By using a 'connector' technique, the authors can achieve high accuracy without the heavy computational cost of solving the full Kohn-Sham equations, making it a promising route for rapid materials design.
main_problem: The challenge of developing simple, explicit, and computationally efficient functionals for electron charge density that depend directly on the Kohn-Sham potential, avoiding the need to solve the full Schrödinger equation.
main_result: The authors demonstrate that a hierarchy of approximations, specifically the 'Connector of the Theory' (COT) approach, can accurately and efficiently approximate charge density in highly inhomogeneous systems like cubic helium.
method: The method uses a 'connector' strategy to replace higher-order Taylor expansion terms of a real system with optimized data from a Homogeneous Electron Gas (HEG) model, utilizing the Lindhard response function.
model_or_system: The study uses the Homogeneous Electron Gas (HEG) as a fundamental building block and tests the developed functionals on cubic helium, a prototypical strongly inhomogeneous solid, under various compression and expansion states.
key_observables: Electron charge density n(r), total electron number (N_el), Hartree energy, and orbital-free kinetic energy functionals (TFvW, PC07).
important_parameters: Kohn-Sham potential (v_KS), Lindhard density-density response function, lattice constants (ranging from 2.5 to 15 Bohr), and the alpha-ansatz parameters (A and B).
main_assumptions: The chemical potential is assumed to be zero (valid for extended systems); the study focuses on the Kohn-Sham potential as a given input rather than including explicit interaction effects in the functional itself.
figures_summary: Figure 1 compares KS potentials with connector potentials; Figure 2 shows Taylor expansion accuracy; Figure 3 compares various density approximations for helium; Figure 5 shows density profiles; Figure 6 shows parameter fitting results; Figure 7 shows density under compression; Figure 9 and 10 show error distributions (MADE) and errors in physical observables.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of density functionals, defines the HEG model and the COT strategy, presents a hierarchy of approximations (LPA to COT1-alpha), tests these against benchmark KS results using cubic helium, evaluates error transferability across different densities, and discusses future extensions to non-local potentials.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Local Kohn-Sham potential along the (001) direction of cubic helium in the LDA, compared to the connector po- tential vc r that would yield exactly the same density, and to the COT1-av and COT1-α approximations for the connector (see Table I). Atoms are situated at the left and right sides of the displayed region. The lower panel is a zoom on the low density region.

FIG. 2. Taylor expansion of the density of a HEG, nh(vh), around vh 0 = −0.5 Hartree (top panel) and around vh 0 = −2.5 Hartree (bottom panel). The orange, green and red curves give the zeroth, first, second order expansions, respectively. The purple line is obtained by evaluating the derivative (the Lindhard function) at the midpoint, following Eq. (7) and the subsequent discussion in the text.

FIG. 3. Charge density of cubic helium resulting from a given LDA KS potential. In black, benchmark result from the so- lution of the KS equation. All other curves are approxima- tions. The horizontal dashed line is the zero-order result, using a homogeneous potential v0r that is the average po- tential of the system. Upper panel: blue dot-dashed, linear response. Blue continuous line: COT1 result, where the lin- ear response is used to approximate the connector potential, Eq. (5). Lower panel: red dot-dashed, linear response with derivative taken at the average of two homogeneous poten- tials to approximate the approach of Cauchy and Lagrange, Eq. (13) used in Eq. (10). Red continuous,...

FIG. 4. Charge density of cubic helium. In black, benchmark result from the solution of the KS equation. All other curves are approximations starting from the LPA, which is the green dashed curve. A linear expansion around the LPA yields the blue continuous line. Black empty squares are the COT1 result, where the linear response is used to approximate the connector potential, Eq. (5). The lower panel zooms into the low-density region.

Figure 4 shows that the first-order correction brings the density on the atoms and its decay closer to the benchmark result, compared to this already significantly improved starting point. Moreover, the density is always positive, as opposed to Fig. 3. Still, the results of linear response are too similar to the LPA to be useful in prac- tice, and COT does not add any significant correction with respect to this outcome. This can be rationalized by looking at the linear-response expression Eq. (1), which is also the basis of COT.
